text: The Qinding Siku Quanshu, in the twelfth category of Zi, contains the following introduction: The Zhongzhao Gushi, a work of two volumes, was compiled by Wei Chi Wo of the Southern Tang Dynasty. The details of Wei Chi Wo's background are not fully known, but the book's preface states that it was written by Wei Chi Wo, who was a historian commissioned to write it during the reign of the Li family, who claimed to be descendants of Emperor Taizong of Tang. Thus, they referred to Chang'an as the "Central Court." This work records the historical anecdotes of the four Tang dynasties of Xuan, Yi, Zhao, and Ai. The first volume mostly documents the deeds of emperors and officials, as well as the systems of the court, while the second volume contains a miscellaneous collection of supernatural and strange events. Some of these accounts cannot be fully relied upon. For example, the story of Emperor Xuanzong being persecuted by Emperor Wuzong and seeking refuge as a monk in Jiangnan has been criticized by Sima Guang's Kao Yi as baseless and unfounded. Another example is the story of Lu Yan plotting against Liu Zhan, which relied on Zhang Gong Su, the governor of Youzhou, to submit a petition to clear the matter. However, Zhang Gong Su was not the governor of Youzhou at that time; it was Zhang Yun Shen. Therefore, the account is in error. Additionally, the story of Zheng Tian's ghost pregnancy is identical to the beginning and end of the story "Qi Tui Nu Chuan" written by a Tang person, but with different names. Despite these inaccuracies, the time of the Southern Tang Dynasty was not far from the Tang Dynasty, so the accounts from the Li family's literature are often credible. For instance, the story of Cui Yanzhao and Wang Ning's enmity, though Sima Guang's Kao Yi points out the error of replacing Yanzhao with Ning in the salt and iron administration, the entire story is taken from this work. Another example is Emperor Zhaozong's poem "Pusa Man," which is often recorded by literary critics and is mostly based on this work. Readers should distinguish and observe separately from the official history, discarding errors and retaining truths, which can still provide valuable evidence.

We, the officials, carefully record that the *Jinhua Zi* in two volumes was written by Liu Chongyuan of the Southern Tang. Liu Chongyuan, originally from Henan, fled the chaos caused by Huang Chao at the end of the Tang Dynasty and moved south. He served the Li family, holding the titles of Wenlin Lang and Dali Sizhi. He admired the character of Huang Chuping and thus gave himself the pen name *Jinhua Zi*, using it as the title for his writings. Liu Chongyuan wrote a self-introduction that provides a brief summary. At the end of the preface, he introduces himself with his official title, but does not specify the date. The book is listed as three volumes in the *Song Yiwenzhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature), but no complete copies have survived. Only fragments can be found in the *Yongle Dadian* (Great Canon of the Yongle Era), from which about sixty entries have been collected. Upon examination, the content of the book records stories from the late Tang period, aligning with what Chao Gongwu said about recording events after the *Dazhong* era. The book covers a wide range of topics, including the virtues and vices of generals and ministers, the strengths and weaknesses of the *fan zhen* (regional military governors), as well as literary works, songs, and stories of supernatural phenomena. These provide valuable complementary evidence to the official histories. The *Zizhi Tongjian* records events such as Emperor Xuanzong's audience with Linghu Tong, Li Jingrang's respect for his mother's teachings, Wang Shifan's appointment as county magistrate, and Wang Shi's control of chaos, all of which are based on this book. Thus, Sima Guang also highly valued it. However, the entry about Liu Xun inheriting the governorship of Yan Prefecture is a bit ambiguous, as it only mentions the governor of Yan.

To investigate the surname Zhang by examining the histories of the Five Dynasties by Ouyang Xiu and Xue Juzheng, at that time the governor of Yan was actually Ge Congzhou, who could not avoid different accounts in hearsay, but in essence, most of what is generally credible is reliable, and differs significantly from those gathered from the streets and alleys. Hu Yinglin, in his *Nine Streams Postscript*, criticized it for being shallow and vulgar. Examining Hu Yinglin, he still considered Chong Yuan's work as not correcting the errors of Zhao, knowing that he had not seen Chong Yuan's preface, and compared it with Liu Ji's *Yu Zi*, Su Boyan's *Kongtong Zi*, and others, thus not recognizing it as a historical record but mistakenly placing it among works of speech. The people of the Ming dynasty were deceitful and fond of making grand statements to deceive, which is not trustworthy. Carefully collate and compile it into two volumes, and place Chong Yuan's original preface at the beginning to preserve its essence. In the ninth month of the forty-sixth year of the Qianlong era, respectfully proofread and presented. General editor: Minister Ji Yun, Minister Lu Xixiong, Minister Sun Shiyi. Chief proofreader: Minister Lu Fei Chi. The *Imperial Library* designated edition Volume One of *Stories of the Middle Court* Compiled by Wei Chi Wo of the Southern Tang Dynasty During the reign of Emperor Da Zhong, he often traveled incognito through the lanes and alleys, riding a donkey with a heavy load, looking around with wide eyes. He would often return to the imperial palace late at night. Many of the inner court officials advised the emperor that it was inappropriate for him to travel so frequently. The emperor replied, "I want to gather information about the customs and practices of the common people. Just as Emperor Ming did not correct internal difficulties before his reign, when he was a prince, he would travel around the southern part of the city, walking through the villages and meeting Wang Ju to discuss matters. This helped him achieve great things. Therefore, I need to visit and observe the people." One day, while on a street, he saw a man who looked like a military officer sitting on a stone bench under a locust tree. When the emperor approached, the man quickly stood up and bowed. The emperor questioned him and learned his surname.

Zhao, a man from Huainan, was asked if he had heard that Du Cong, the chancellor, was being dispatched to govern Huainan and wanted to pay respects to him. The emperor asked if Zhao was an old acquaintance. Zhao replied that he was not. He then went to present himself. The emperor asked, "Have you heard what kind of person Du Cong is?" Zhao replied, "Du Cong is a statesman who has served multiple emperors. It is not by chance that the emperor, being wise, has entrusted him with such a position." The emperor was pleased and inquired, "What do you have in your chest?" Zhao replied, "It is a document detailing my actions." The emperor kept Zhao and instructed, "Just stay in the lodging and wait. Du Cong will surely come to summon you." The next day, the emperor gave the document to the chancellor of Bin, who then wrote, "Appoint as the special envoy to Huainan." Zhao would receive a generous salary for life. People praised Zhao for his good fortune. Emperor Xuanzong was the youngest son of Emperor Xianzong. His elder brother was Emperor Muzong. After Emperor Muzong, Emperor Wenzong and Emperor Wuzong succeeded to the throne. Emperor Xuanzong was their uncle. Emperor Wuzong, upon ascending the throne, deeply feared someone. One day, while playing in the imperial gardens, Emperor Wuzong summoned the emperor. From afar, he saw a prince who could dismount, and Cao Shiliang, the eunuch, leaped forward on his horse and said, "Just saw a prince who can dismount." Cao Shiliang ordered the eunuch to carry the emperor out, and the army reported that the prince had fallen off his horse and could not be saved. Soon, the emperor became a monk and wandered in the southern regions. In the later years of the Xiantong era, the eunuchs requested his return to the capital, and he ascended the throne. Emperor Xizong was born in the third year of the Xiantong era and ascended the throne on the nineteenth day of the seventh month in the fourteenth year, at the age of twelve. The left army commander, Tian Lingzi, supported him in the court. Emperor Xizong called him "Father" and ruled the court himself, with no one daring to speak. Each time he entered to present his views, he would prepare two trays of fruits and snacks and converse with the emperor in a relaxed manner before retiring, which became a regular practice. After several years, he accompanied the emperor to Sichuan, where he became increasingly cruel and killed many people. When the imperial procession returned to the capital, he did not dare to accompany them. At that time, Tian Lingzi saw Chen Jingxuan as the governor of Xichuan and sought to become a military supervisor, but died before achieving this goal.

Emperor Zhaozong, the younger brother of Emperor Xizong, was born in the year Dinghai of the Xiantong era (867). He ascended the throne in the third month of the first year of the Wende era (888) at the age of twenty-two. He was known for his dignified and clear appearance, and his reputation was highly esteemed. Despite the difficult and perilous times of his reign, his intelligence and capacity were exceptional. He frequently engaged in discussions and debates about state affairs with his ministers without ever tiring. In the third year of the Ganning era, Li Maozhen of Fengxiang had a conflict with the court officials and was planning to cause trouble and threaten the capital. Emperor Zhaozong decided to move to Taiyuan to avoid the conflict. When he stopped in Huazhou, Han Jian welcomed him back to the city. Emperor Zhaozong felt depressed and unhappy and often climbed to the western part of the city to gaze at the clouds. In the following autumn, he composed two poems in the style of "Pusamazhen": One poem said: "Climbing a tower to look far into the distance at the palaces of Qin, I see only two flying swallows. The Wei River flows through countless mountains and hills. Distant smoke covers the green path, and travelers wonder where they are going. It is the heroes who welcome their families back to the old palace." Another poem said: "Drifting in the shade of the Three Peaks, the autumn wind often brings tears. Heartbreak and longing for the fairy palace in the hazy smoke and mist."

Dreaming often, he slept without speaking, appearing as if intoxicated. Whether early or late, the question of when one would return home lingers, and only the heavens know. In the Wu Wu year, the emperor returned to the capital. In the Geng Shen year, due to the many corrupt eunuchs, he wished to remove those in power. On the fifth day of the eleventh month, the Left Army Commander Liu Jishu and the Right Army Commander Wang Zhongxian, along with the Chief of Staff Wang Yanfan and Xue Qiwo, led the imperial guards and summoned officials from various regions to present a fake petition requesting the emperor to abdicate for his own well-being. They forced him out of the eastern palace and installed a regent. In the following year, on the first day of the first month, the emperor was restored to power, and four key figures were executed. The era name was changed to Tian Fu. In the eleventh month, Zhu Quanzhong led his troops into Hezhong. By the fourth month of winter, the emperor was again surrounded by soldiers from Fengxiang and taken to Zhengcheng. Zhu Quanzhong led his troops to meet the emperor and besieged him, a situation that lasted for three years. In the Gui Hai year, on the twenty-second day of the first month, the emperor left Zhu Quanzhong's camp and returned to the capital. In the Ji Zi year, Zhu Quanzhong welcomed the emperor to Luoyang. In the fourth month, the era name was changed to Tian You. On the eleventh day of the eighth month, Zhu Quanzhong committed usurpation, causing great suffering throughout the empire. The Jingzhao Prefect holds the power of life and death, yet high-ranking officials are often viewed with disdain. He is responsible for overseeing the affairs of the capital and the country. Scholars who pass the imperial examinations and become known are quickly placed on a clear path to advancement, anticipating important roles. If they reach the highest levels of government, they must have experience in serving the people, often passing through Chang'an. Even if the emperor is in the eastern part of Luoyang, they serve as the governor of Luoyang. After a few months, they are sure to be transferred to the inner chambers of the government, a position unmatched by any other. County magistrates, upon taking up their duties, must pay respects to the Jingzhao Prefect, who treats them with exceptional courtesy. Mats and cushions are laid out in the courtyard, and officials lead one person to present a business card to the prefect, who then performs a two-bow ceremony. The prefect descends from the western corridor to welcome them, and they are invited to a meal in the government office, a practice that has become a precedent.

During the reign of Emperor Xianzong (咸通中), Liu Zhan, serving as a high-ranking official (中书侍郎平章事), maintained himself through frugality and integrity, and assisted the era with loyalty and righteousness. After the death of Princess Tongchang (懿皇以同昌公主薨谢), Emperor Yizong was angered by the physicians Han Zongshao and others, and imprisoned them in the Cold Platform (霜台), along with over 200 of their relatives, scatteredly in the Dali Temple (散系大理). Both inside and outside the court were in fear and anxiety. Liu Zhan submitted a stern and urgent remonstration (上防切谏). At this time, Wei Shangguan and Lu Yan, relying on their favor and envying him, dismissed Liu Zhan as the military governor of Jingnan (出瞻为荆南节度使), causing widespread dissatisfaction (中外咸不平之). Zheng Tian, a翰林学士, wrote an official edict (翰林承防郑畋为制词), which stated: "Early on, he excelled in literature and passed the imperial examinations with distinction, had a very high reputation for integrity, was cautious and untainted by scandal, was particularly known for his frugality and integrity, did not value superficial elegance, lived in a modest home, which was not even his own property, and rejected bribes from all directions, only fearing that others would know." Wei and Lu were greatly angered (韦路大怒) and demoted Zheng Tian to the position of governor of Wuzhou (贬畋为梧州刺史), checking the map and finding that Huanzhou was over ten thousand miles from the capital (取十道图检见驩州去京万里), and exiled Liu Zhan to the position of Household Affairs Officer in Huanzhou (谪瞻为驩州司戸参军). Li Yu, an official, wrote an official edict (舍人李庾行诰词), criticizing Liu Zhan severely (驳责深焉), intending to harm him (将欲加害). At this time, Emperor Yizong had passed away (时遇懿皇厌代), and Emperor Xizong ascended the throne (僖皇初立), appointing the veteran minister Xiao Fang to assist in major state affairs (用元臣萧仿佐佑大政). Xiao Fang recommended Liu Zhan to take his place (仿举瞻自代), and Zhang Gong Su, the military governor of Youzhou, submitted a defense of Liu Zhan (幽州节度使张公素上防理之), halting Wei and Lu's intentions (韦路意乃止焉). Soon after, Lu Yan was appointed as the military governor of Yizhou (俄而路岩出为益帅), and Wei Shangguan left his position as chancellor (保衡又离相位). Liu Zhan was called back to serve as the governor of Kangzhou (召瞻为康州刺史), and later appointed as the governor of Guozhou (再授虢州). Liu Zhan returned to the Xiang River (旋至湘江), where Wei Shangguan fled south (韦保衡南窜), and they met on the river (相遇于江中). Liu Zhan's family members boarded the boat and openly insulted Wei Shangguan (瞻家人齐登舟外诟骂之), while Wei Shangguan restrained his family members and could not respond (保衡约束家人无辞以对). Wei Shangguan was executed in the posthouse of Huzhou (至贺州驿内伏法), which was the same person who had killed Yang Shou's son on the bed several years ago (乃是数年前杀杨收閤子中榻上也). Liu Zhan arrived in Hunan (瞻至湖南), where Li Yu, who was then governing the region, met him at the bamboo plaque pavilion by the river (李庾方典是郡出迎于江次竹牌亭), held a banquet, and sang the *Zhuzhi Ci* to bid farewell to Li Yu (置酒瞻唱竹枝词送李庾), crossing the ditch with shoes on (蹑履过沟). The *Zhuzhi Ci* expressed deep resentment (竹枝恨渠深), and "daughter" referred to a young woman, possibly a reference to the song's theme (女儿).

Yǔ, angry, poured wine for Zhan. Zhan ordered Yǔ to respond with poetry. Yǔ said he did not understand the nuances of poetry and music. Zhan threw down his cup and said, "You should only be able to compose official documents." That night, Yǔ drank poison and died. Zhan arrived in the capital and soon entered the Office of State Affairs. At the time, the Prime Minister Liu Yè had previously been acquainted with Wei Lù and showed deep concern. While presiding over the Salt and Iron Department, he set a trap in the courtyard, invited Zhan to drink, and poisoned him, causing Zhan's death. Liu Yè was soon appointed Governor of Huainan. Emperor Xī held a banquet in the Lín Dé Hall. A court musician composed a song that said, "Duke Liu will go to Yangzhou to oversee affairs, and the people's suffering will surely be alleviated." All the musicians echoed in response, "This is truly the Medicine King Bodhisattva!" Everyone laughed at it. Lù Yán was immediately exiled to Dān Prefecture. The people seized his property in Jīng Prefecture, and the amount was beyond measure. There was a mosquito net, light and dense like blue smoke, which people suspected was made of fish scales. And he was executed in Xīn Prefecture. During the Xī Tōng era, the Chancellor Cui Yànzhāo and the Minister of War Wáng Níng were outwardly brothers. Wáng Níng passed the imperial examination in the first year of Dà Zhōng. The following year, Cui Yànzhāo still failed the examination. He visited Wáng Níng, who dressed formally to meet him. Cui was very angry. Wáng Níng then teased him, saying, "You should just take the *Míng Jīng* examination." Cui Yànzhāo left in anger. Three years later, he passed the examination. In the Yì Huáng era, many officials who served as Deputy Minister of War and oversaw the Salt and Iron Department went on to hold high positions. One day, Wáng Níng was appointed to this office and decided to enter the prime ministerial role. After Cui Yànzhāo's downfall, several months later, there were problems in the Salt and Iron Department. Wáng Níng was dismissed from his position, and the court appointed Cui Yànzhāo to take his place. Half a year later, Cui Yànzhāo became prime minister. Cui Yànzhāo's mother ordered the making of many shoes and told her maidservants, "Wáng's sister will surely be exiled along with Wáng Langyuan, and I want to accompany my little sister on the journey." Cui Yànzhāo heard this and wept, bowing to his mother, who said, "There will surely be no such thing." Wáng Níng was ultimately spared from blame.

In ancient times, the five officials responsible for the five elements (wood, fire, earth, metal, and water) did not fail in their duties and could distinguish all sounds, colors, smells, and tastes. The noble statesman and scholar, Li Deyu, while serving in the government, had a close friend who was sent on a mission to Jingkou. Li Deyu said, "When you return, bring back a jar of water from the Zhongling Spring at the foot of Jinshan Mountain on the Yangtze River." The friend, while rowing, got drunk and forgot to bring back the water. Later, when he reached Shicheng Mountain, he remembered and brought back a jar of water from the Yangtze River. Upon returning to the capital and presenting it to Li Deyu, he was surprised and said, "The taste of the water from the Yangtze River is different from what it was a few years ago. This water is quite similar to the water from Shicheng Mountain in Jianye." The friend apologized and did not hide the truth. Another friend was appointed as the governor of Shuzhou. Li Deyu said to him, "When you arrive in that prefecture, bring back a few catties of tea from Mount Tianzhu." The friend brought back dozens of catties, but Li Deyu refused to accept it and returned it. The next year, after the friend left office, he sought out the tea with great care and brought back a few catties. Li Deyu accepted it and said, "This tea can neutralize the toxicity of alcohol and meat." He ordered it to be boiled and poured over a dish of meat, which he then sealed in a silver container. The next morning, when he opened it, the meat had turned to water. Everyone marveled at Li Deyu's broad knowledge. As for the legend, it is said that there is a spring in the sea that flows through the kingdom of Silla, and its water is clear and sweet. However, if the kingdom neglects to offer it to China, the water becomes murky and tasteless. After Emperor Xuanzong of Tang fled to Sichuan during the An Lushan Rebellion, people in Jiangnan rarely tasted the fruits of Lingnan. Similarly, during the year 874, when Emperor Xizong was in Sichuan, the citrus fruits from Dongting Lake, the pears from Luoyang, and the persimmons from Muren all tasted sour and bitter. The officials in the northern part of the capital, who were responsible for making up for deficiencies, often had to apologize and swear oaths when they presented their reports. The right Censor-in-Attendance and the left Remonstrator would often form joint documents, and thus, the officials in the capital highly regarded the right Censor-in-Attendance and the left Remonstrator.

Former chancellors of the previous dynasty rarely avoided being demoted, but Xu Shang held office as a high-ranking official for several decades without any trouble. His son, Qi Guo Gong Yan Ruo, was also loyal to the emperor and harmonious with the people, and thus never faced any demotion. Lu Dan, after passing the imperial examination, served as both a general and a chancellor for 49 years, without taking credit for his achievements, attributing them to others. The chancellor's dining hall food was considered too prestigious for ordinary people to eat. When Zheng Yanchang was in the position of chancellor, one day he wanted to eat, and his younger brother Zheng Yanji came, so they ate together. Zheng Yanji held a bowl of noodles, but it fell from his hand, and he suffered a stroke, dying the same night. The Chief Rite Minister initially presented the complete performance of refined music in the inner court, while the Deputy Rite Minister presented only half of it. The children of officials were all afraid to marry princesses, as the emperor's relatives were powerful and influential. Princesses appointed their own officials, and their estates, mansions, and carriages were staffed by many servants. Each princess had her own courtyard and living quarters, and they gathered with relatives for feasts or outings. The husbands of princesses were not allowed to see them, and servants dared not look at them. Princesses acted as they pleased, often not seeing their husbands for several days. Only Yu Cong, the chancellor, who married Guangde Princess, was virtuous and harmonious, and she was the sister of the revered emperor. Yu Cong was pursued by Wei Lu and they both went to Zhaozhou. Yu Cong was repeatedly given a potion by an imperial eunuch, and Guangde Princess scolded him fiercely, snatching it and throwing it away. The chancellor held his belt and sat. All the officials in the places they passed through dared not greet them, and they were carried on their shoulders and walked side by side. Eventually, Yu Cong received an imperial decree to return to the capital, where he was appointed as a tutor to the crown prince and later as the Right Chancellor. It was said that this was due to the influence of the princess.

In the imperial gardens and palaces, there are many names for the eight seasonal outings and tours. Every year when the cherries ripen, the two armies each choose a day to arrange feasts and await the emperor's visit, which is called the entourage. The grand performance of music and songs continues throughout the day, with various forms of entertainment, including acrobatics, music, and dance, and all kinds of food and drink, both from land and water, are fully prepared. Cherries are piled up everywhere to be admired and enjoyed. In Tongzhou, there is the Changchun Palace, where the gardens and parks are lush, and all kinds of flowers and trees are present. The fragrance and beauty last as long as the spring season. Behind the Zhongshu Sheng (Chancellery) are five offices, and there are fifteen officials in these offices. Each year, they collect fifteen thousand strings of cash for brewing and social gatherings. In autumn, they rent four large houses in the city, arranged in a row, to prepare and decorate conveniently. They plant flowers and herbs everywhere. When the peonies bloom, they invite the four prime ministers to come, along with family members and relatives. High-ranking officials are welcomed every day, and the imperial music and dance troupe provides entertainment. Imperial gifts of wine and food are received without a single day passing without such events. Eunuchs drive high carriages and large horses to arrive, to receive gold and silk as rewards. The events continue until the flowers fall and the event ends.

After the retreat of Huang Chao, the capital repaired the damaged areas. There was a man from the Wang family in Zhenzhou, nicknamed Wang Jiuhu, who lived in the capital and had a vast fortune. He contributed thirty thousand strings of cash to assist in the repair of the Zhuyue Gate. The emperor then ordered the reconstruction of An Guo Temple to be completed and personally descended in a carriage to set up a grand vegetarian feast. The new bell was struck twelve times, and one million strings of cash were donated by the emperor, allowing the various ministers to strike the bell according to their own will. The emperor said, "Anyone who can donate one thousand strings of cash will strike the bell once." After the feast, Wang Jiuhu, half-drunk, entered and went straight to the bell tower, striking it one hundred times. He then transported ten million strings of cash from the Western Market into the temple. Han Jian, protecting his mother, searched for a burial site. A fortune-teller said there was only one grave site that could accommodate a large amount of money and goods, but it would not last long. If it were within the boundaries of Huazhou, there would be none better. Han Jian then buried his mother there. The following year, the imperial procession visited the Three Peaks, and people from all over the country gathered like spokes around a hub. Han Jian then widely collected commercial taxes. Two years later, he had a visible sum of nine million strings of cash. Three years later, all of it came under the control of Zhu Quanzhong. The two military forces established street patrols to prohibit violent and cruel behavior within the military. If commoners fought or stole, it belonged to the capital's administration and the two county's criminal investigation departments. Soldiers and commoners did not mix, and this was also the case throughout the country. On both sides of the main street, there were willow trees, commonly known as the "Haiya" and "Liuya," meaning they formed rows like officials lined up in court. Every year on the third day of the third lunar month, palace maids were allowed to meet with their relatives in front of the Great Harmony Hall in Xingqing Palace. They could inquire about their families and exchange gifts. Within one day, there were millions of people. Some relatives met immediately upon arrival, while others called out names by evening but did not come, leaving in tears. This happened every year.

Within the hot springs of Huaqing Palace, during the Tianbao era, stones were carved to form seats and the "Furong Wen," which still exist today. The wooden structures are also largely intact. Lishan is home to many birds known as "Alan Dui." Emperor Xuanzong played his jade flute, capturing their sounds to compose a tune, which became famous. Courtiers and officials all sang and played this tune, spreading it far and wide. People competed to play it on their flutes, and the poet Zhang You wrote: "The red trees rustle, the palace gates half open, where the former emperor once visited. To this day, the custom in Lishan is for village flutes to still play 'Alan Dui.'" **Middle Kingdom Anecdotes, Volume I** **Imperially Approved Complete Library** **Middle Kingdom Anecdotes, Volume II** **Compiled by Wei Chi Wu of the Southern Tang Dynasty** ### Ancient Tales There was a legendary figure known as the Dragon Raiser. In Chang'an, there was a family or person who raised dragons. By observing the water, one could know the color, type, and presence of dragons. During the reign of a revered emperor, a dragon-raising family reported that two dragons had escaped from the dragon pool. They searched for the dragons in the Guandong region for dozens of days. The dragons were found in the Huiwang Pool in the Eastern Capital (Luoyang). They were captured and returned to the imperial palace. When they passed through Huazhou, Li Ne was the governor of Huazhou. His father, Jian Shao, was previously on good terms with Bai Juyi. Li Ne was an upright person. Upon hearing about the dragons, he considered it a falsehood and ordered them to be brought to the government office for inspection. They were placed in a small bottle and then poured into a basin, where they turned out to be two small eels. Li Ne angrily asked how this could be verified. The person replied that verification was not difficult and suggested digging a hole one foot wide in the ground. Water was then poured into the hole. The eels were thrown into the water and began to swim around, their tails touching the corners of the hole, causing it to expand and the water to rise. The hole had widened to several feet. The person advised Li Ne that if the hole continued to widen, it would be difficult to control. The eels were then placed back into the bottle. Li Ne was surprised and gave generous gifts of money and silk before taking the eels back to the imperial palace.

During the late period of Emperor Wuzong's reign (Da Zhong), Xu Yanruo's younger brother, Xu Yanshu, encountered someone at the Lantern Festival in the alleys of the capital. As night deepened, a man approached Xu Yanshu with a formal greeting and walked with him. The man said, "You are a person of great fortune; your elder brother will surely become a high-ranking official in the future. However, if he remains in office for nearly ten years, he should request retirement and go to a place five thousand miles away from the capital to avoid calamity. Otherwise, he will face misfortune." They reached a small alley, and the man said, "I live in this alley; please visit me another day," and then they parted ways. After ten days, Xu Yanshu returned to the alley and found no one living there. He walked another ten steps and discovered a small shrine at the end of the road. He visited the shrine and saw a clay figure that resembled the man he had met the previous night. The figure smiled at him, and Xu Yanshu remembered the encounter. By the late period of Emperor Zhaozong's reign (Guang Hua), Xu Yanshu had risen to the position of Left Censor, while his elder brother was serving as a high minister. They discussed the matter, and at that time, the empire was in turmoil, with powerful figures controlling all directions except for the southern region, which was governed by Prince Xue's heir, Zhao Ruo, as the military governor. Xu Yanruo then requested to be appointed as the military governor of Guangzhou. Emperor Zhaozong granted him the authority, and he went to Guangzhou, thereby avoiding the impending disaster.

During the reign of Emperor Xuan, a Taoist named Dong Yuansu arrived from the south, and people said he could command ghosts and spirits. The emperor heard about him and summoned him for an audience. His appearance was very unusual, and the emperor remarked to those around him, "This person is unfathomable." He was kept in the Hanlin Academy for the night. Later that night, the emperor called him for a conversation and said, "I have heard that you possess considerable magical skills. Now, in the south, oranges and tangerines are ripe. Can you bring them here?" Dong Yuansu replied, "It's a small matter. Please place one box before the emperor's bed." After a few moments, a slight breeze entered the tent, and Dong Yuansu then opened the box. It was filled with oranges. He reported, "These are oranges from Zhijiang County in Jiangling. It would take too long to get them from afar." The emperor tasted them and was greatly amazed, saying, "You should be able to get anything you want." Dong Yuansu replied, "If it were not by divine mandate, how dare I take it for myself? Taking it for oneself would surely bring divine retribution." The next day, the emperor ordered a young noble to play a silver flute. At night, on a high tree, the emperor called Dong Yuansu to listen to the sound calmly. The emperor said, "There is a strange thing nearby; can you chase it away for me?" Dong Yuansu smiled and said, "It's just a child." He then wrote a talisman and sent it flying. In a moment, the sound was no longer heard. The next day, the emperor called the child to ask, "While blowing, it seemed like someone was removing something black from his mouth."

The emperor had no way to obtain it. He then gave more than ten pieces to someone to hold and blow at the top of a tree. Xuan Yuan Su listened as Yuan Su exhaled a little, and the sound stopped. The emperor was repeatedly surprised by this. The next day, he called for questioning. It turned out that it was blown by the wind outside the palace wall, making it impossible to reach. The emperor then ordered the creation of an underground chamber in the rear garden, similar in size to a house, with a candle placed inside. Several people were instructed to beat drums and tell Yuan Su that there was another demon, asking him to chase it away. Yuan Su flew a talisman, and after a long time, the sound disappeared. The next day, the emperor reprimanded the people in the underground chamber, saying that when they were beating the drums, the imperial messenger came to stop them from doing so. That evening, the underground chamber was made to sound again, with instructions to continue even if they heard the announcement. No sound was heard again. The next day, the emperor asked for the reason, and the response was that the previous night, the emperor personally arrived at the underground chamber and ordered them to stop, so they dared not disobey. The emperor said that tonight they should continue, even if he came, and there should be no stopping. No sound was heard again. The next day, it was reported that a red dragon entered the underground chamber, and everyone was startled and ran away, explaining why. The emperor was extremely frightened and showed great respect. The various magical arts performed cannot all be recorded, and the rewards given were numerous. After half a year, Yuan Su firmly requested to return to Jiangnan and was finally allowed to go, and his fate is unknown.

Li Siqi, a man who often wore a green hat and sat on a mat in the capital, appeared to be in his thirties. Whenever he visited the market or entered taverns, he befriended everyone he met. Linghu Chu heard about him and invited him to his home, where his speech was not that of an ordinary person. Linghu Chu's son, Tong, stood by and also felt his strangeness, noting that he claimed to stay at the Great Heaven Temple. The next day, Linghu Chu sent someone to look for him but found no trace. During the Xiantong era, Tong became the governor of Huainan and had been in that position for over thirty years. A gatekeeper saw Siqi in the market and recognized him as he was then, surprising and reporting to Tong. Tong was also surprised and sent someone to invite him, who was then treated as a mentor. Siqi asked Tong, "Why do you look so aged?" Tong bowed twice and asked him to stay for the night, but Siqi said he was staying at the Purple Extreme Palace and left without returning. Someone else saw him again on a building and Tong sent someone to find him, but he did not return, and no one knew where he went. Jia Wenchang, during the Zhenyuan era, was a guest and follower of Wang Weigao of Nankang in Western Sichuan. After Wang Weigao's death, he suffered under Liu Pi and became an official in a remote county.

Gao Chongwen was recalled to his former position in Jian'nan, and Wen Chang repeatedly thanked him. Gao Chongwen said, "You are not destined to remain in a lowly position." Pointing to the chair under him, he said to Wen Chang, "This chair is still not worthy for you to sit on." He immediately requested to return to the capital. Traveling to Xingyuan, he arrived at a mountain temple where an old monk pointed to the plum tree in front of the courtyard and said to Wen Chang, "When you left, you met the blooming plum blossoms; when you return, you should see the apricot blossoms in bloom." Upon arriving in the capital, Wen Chang was promoted several times. Several years later, he was appointed the military governor of Yizhou. Passing through Xingyuan to the former temple of the monk, he saw the apricot blossoms in full bloom in the courtyard. Visiting the monk, who had already passed away, Wen Chang remembered him with deep emotion and felt sorrowful, setting up a memorial service before leaving. Wen Chang's son Anjie was a heavy and substantial person whose words were never empty or false. He often said that during the Tianfu period, he fled the chaos and left the capital, arriving at an inn in Shangshan. There, he saw an old woman with no half of her head, seated on a bed, weaving hemp with her hands very skillfully. Her daughter-in-law was by her side, saying that during the Guangming Gengzi year, the rebel Chao entered the capital and was injured by the rebels.

A man had half of his nose and above cut away, and someone treated him with medicine, wrapping the wound. He did not die for two days, but then passed away as usual. Afterward, he slowly began to move his hands and feet, and his family members fed him rice gruel, and after a long time, he was unharmed. It has been more than twenty years since then, and there are such strange events in the world. Anjie also said that there are many haunted houses in Chang'an that no one dares to live in. In the east street, there is a house where in the hall there is a green-faced figure, resembling indigo, with eyes like fire, occupying the entire five-room hall. People call it "Big Green Face." In the west street, there is a house where in the "Gui Tóufáng" (a small room) there is also a green-faced figure, fitting into one room. People call it "Little Green Face." During a cold festival, Anjie, a young man, and his companions were playing with a ball and accidentally entered this house. They were careful not to go too far from the wall and saw something in the grass. Anjie and his companions broke through the wall to retrieve the ball, and in a few steps, they peeked into the room and saw the green-faced figure, which filled the entire house. Its eyes roamed over the people, and they all ran out at once, too frightened to take the ball.

During the Xiantong era, there was a conjurer whose name is unknown, who performed tricks in the streets. He brought along a boy who was ten years old or older, and with a knife, he cut off the boy’s head, placing it on the ground and then reattaching it. He then begged for money, saying, “This child is alive.” The crowd eagerly gave him money. He then shouted, and the boy immediately jumped up. The next day, he performed the same trick again, gathering thousands of people and earning a great deal of money. But later, when he shouted, the boy did not rise. The conjurer then apologized to the onlookers, saying, “I have just arrived in the capital and have not yet had the chance to pay respects. All the masters here have made this small trick impossible to perform. I beg you to forgive me—I will take you as my master.” After saying this, he shouted again, but the boy did not rise. Soon after, a patrol official arrested him, saying, “You have killed a person; you must go to the public office.” The conjurer replied, “Among a thousand people, I would certainly not escape, but I have another strange skill—please allow me to demonstrate it.” It is not too late to proceed with the law. He then took a small ball from a case, cut open his arm, and inserted the ball inside. He then tried again to revive the boy, but it was ineffective. Shortly after, he revealed his arm, and a small sweet ball had already grown on it. He said, “I do not wish to kill anyone. I beg the masters to let this child rise—it would be a great favor.” He shouted again, but the boy did not rise. The conjurer sighed and said, “I cannot avoid killing him.” He then cut off the sweet ball and shouted once more. The boy immediately rose as before. Among the crowd, a monk’s head suddenly fell to the ground. The conjurer then gathered all his props and the boy into a cloth bag, tied it to his back, and turned his face upward, exhaling a stream of breath like a ribbon of silk that shot upward into the sky. He suddenly reached up and climbed upward for more than ten feet before vanishing. He then disappeared from sight. The monk was found with his head and body separated.

At Ximing Temple, there was a monk named De Zhen who crossed the sea intending to go to Silla. The boat reached the coast of an island in the sea to shelter from the wind, and he traveled with a companion through the islands. They saw a spring with a red carp inside. A Taoist tried to catch it but failed, then recited a spell and performed the *Yubu* ritual to obtain it. The monk said, "Strange creatures of the sea cannot be confined." The Taoist replied, "I fear no sea god." The monk pleaded and finally threw it back into the waves. Then he went to the East Sea. The next year, the monk returned to the capital and stayed at Ximing Temple again. He then became skilled in divination and prediction, never failing. From then on, he was sought after like a market. In one or two years, he amassed wealth beyond count. One day, a guest visited and saw a small pine shrine filled with banners and flowers. The guest reached in and found a small child, several inches long, wearing red clothes and a red crown, with painted-like features, as if about to speak, then suddenly slipped from his hand and flew away into the air, disappearing. The monk sighed and lamented for a long time, then cursed and chased the guest away. The guest fled in fear and avoided him for a month, later hearing that the monk claimed the event was baseless. Wang Wei, the brother of Ning, was skilled in many strange arts, well-known, and wise. As an envoy sent to Xuan Prefecture to investigate matters, he said to Wei, "What do you have to offer as a farewell gift?" Wei took out a small bag.

There was something like a ball of clay, not knowing what it was, and he called it, saying it should be kept close by, never to be forgotten. After arriving in Xuanzhou and handling affairs for over a month, he slept during the day in the post station. In his sleep, he turned over and was struck by an arrow in the side, feeling intense pain and jumping out of bed. He looked outside and saw the ceiling beam suddenly break and fall onto the bed. The sound of the falling beam was deafening, frightening everyone in the post station and saving him from this disaster. Kang Peng's *Jutalanlu* also records that Wei had a strange technique. The Prime Minister Wei Zhaodu was holding the balance of power, and thousands of people inside and outside the court flocked to him. Suddenly, an old monk came to pay respects, saying, "Your lordship's disaster is about to come. Can you follow me? I have come especially to welcome you." Zhaodu suddenly turned pale and was very frightened, telling the monk, "I have been in power for a long time and deeply fear that disaster will come. But I need to say goodbye to my family." The monk refused, insisting that he enter. When he reached the hall, the old and young gathered and cried, saying it was an untrustworthy lie, pulling at his clothes. After some time, Zhaodu managed to escape and head to the Daoist temple. The monk was already gone, and he asked the guards, but none had seen him. Within two months, he met disaster along with his cousin Li Qi, and their families were destroyed.

It is said that Zheng Tian was born of a ghostly womb. After his mother's death, his father, Ya, remarried and subsequently gave birth to Zheng Tian. Initially, Ya was not well-known and traveled to various places, leaving his wife and a maid in a temple in the mountains, near a nunnery. Upon his return, he found his wife had already died. He asked the maid, who said that the lady was about to give birth. That night, while she was lying down, she heard a voice from the air saying, "You must leave the temple grounds to avoid defiling our sacred space; otherwise, I will kill you." The wife prayed, "I am just a woman, with no place to go. I hope the holy one will take pity on me." By the fifth watch, she gave birth to Zheng Tian, and then she died. The monks in the temple buried her outside the wall in a field. Ya went to pour wine and offer money to her grave. That night, he dreamed that his wife said, "My life is not yet over; I should have given birth to a noble son with you. I was killed by the spirits for defiling the temple. From here, go ten miles south to a monastery. There is only one monk, about fifty years old, who is an extraordinary person. You should seek him out. The monk will initially refuse, but if you persistently plead and pray, you will eventually receive what you seek."

Upon waking, he did not believe it to be true. The next night, he dreamed it again, and the words were the same as before. Subsequently, he hastened to the courtyard where he encountered the Buddhist monk. Initially, the monk was pleased upon meeting him, but when informed, he paid no heed, saying, "I am just an ordinary person who happened to take vows; how could I oversee matters of the unseen realm?" Yet, he persisted in his earnest plea. The monk, angered, struck him with his staff, and he endured the humiliation, refusing to leave for several days. He did not sleep at night either. Finally, the monk consented, saying, "Since your resolve is so firm, wait for me to seek it out. Then I will sit in meditation." After sitting in meditation until midnight, he rose and told him, "The matter is settled. In the morning, just go home first; I will send him." That night, he returned home and observed at the third watch, hearing voices outside the door. He then led his wife to the place, where she said, "My body has already decayed; this is my spirit. Be good and protect it, and then leave." The monk departed, and the events unfolded as if they were part of a lifetime, but he hated being seen in the light. Within three or two years, he gave birth to a child named Tian. After several more years, his wife departed, saying, "The years are up, and it is time to depart." They wept and parted, and soon she was nowhere to be found. In the early Xiantong era, there was a commoner named Cuan, whose name was forgotten, who arrived in the capital by carriage, claiming to have come from the region of Qian and Wu. He visited the residence of a noble official and brought thirty pounds of sheep-tail charcoal. He took out a small saw and a small axe, cutting and shaping the charcoal into two stories, completing it in a few moments. He then spread powdered medicine on top and bottom and burned it with fire. The medicine ignited the fire, and in a moment, it lit up the entire two-story structure. Bright and brilliant, it seemed to have windows, doors, carved pillars, and intricate railings, all meticulously crafted. There was even a flying bridge connecting the two stories, with figures of men and women moving about. After a while, the charcoal gradually flew away, turning to ash, and nothing remained to be seen. The emperor heard of it and summoned him into the imperial palace, but he was soon nowhere to be found.

Li Cong, serving as the governor of Hunan, received a carp presented by a fisherman. The carp was several feet long. Li Cong ordered his family to cook it. In the carp's belly, they found a seal. The inscription read "Hengshan County Seal." Li Cong then instructed his guards to search for recent documents from Hengshan County. Upon examining the seal, it was clearly recognized. He then sent for the magistrate of Hengshan County to bring the seal. When the magistrate arrived, the seal was indeed newly cast. Li Cong questioned him in private. The magistrate confessed guilt and said, "The old seal was stolen by a bad person. I and the chief clerk were both worried about punishment, so we secretly ordered the craftsmen to make it. Now we only await the death sentence." Li Cong pitied him and kept the matter secret. He broke the new seal and ordered the magistrate to carry the old seal back to the county. Few knew about it. Duke Du Cong, a minister, enjoyed a rare combination of good fortune and longevity. He ate five meals a day, which was his usual routine. Each day's expenses amounted to ten thousand coins. At night, he had one meal. In his later years, a physician consulted him, saying, "Your Excellency should not eat at night, as it may cause blockage in your organs and lead to illness." Li Cong laughed and replied, "For over sixty years, I have done this. What harm could it do?" A guest from the western part of the capital saw people herding sheep everywhere, filling the hills and valleys, numbering in the tens of thousands. The guest asked where they came from. They replied, "We come from Fu and Xia to provide food for Your Excellency." As the guest looked around, everything disappeared in an instant, revealing that it was a divine gift.

钦定四库全书 子部十二 提要 中朝故事 小説家一【杂事之属臣】等谨案中朝故事二卷南唐尉迟偓撰偓履贯未详书首旧题朝议郎守给事中修国史骁骑尉赐紫金鱼袋臣尉迟偓奉防纂进盖李氏有国时偓为史官承命所作李昪自以为出太宗之后承唐统绪故称长安为中朝也其书皆记唐宣懿昭哀四朝旧闻上卷多君臣事迹及朝廷制度下卷则杂録神异怪幻之事中间不可尽据者如宣宗为武宗所忌请为僧防行江表一事司马光通 鉴考异已斥其鄙妄无稽又路岩倾害刘瞻赖幽州节度使张公素上疏申理一事考是时镇幽州者乃张允伸非张公素所记殊误又郑畋胎一事与唐人所作齐推女首尾全同而变其姓名尢显出蹈袭然其时去唐未逺故家文献所记亦往往足徴如崔彦昭王凝相仇一事司马光考异虽摘其以彦昭代凝领盐铁之误而其事则全取之又昭宗菩萨蛮词选家所録亦率本于此读者茍与正史分别防观去讹存是固未尝不足以资防证也

金华子 小説家一【杂事之属臣】等谨案金华子二卷南唐刘崇逺撰崇逺家本河南唐末避黄巢之乱渡江南徙仕李氏为文林郎大理司直尝慕皇初平之为人自号金华子因以为所著书名崇逺有自序一篇颇具梗概序末题名具官称臣不着年月而书中所称烈祖髙皇帝者乃南唐先主李昪庙号又有升元受命之语亦南唐中主李景纪年晁公武读书志乃以为唐人陈振孙书録解题则泛指为五代人宋濂诸 子辨则并谓其人不可考诸説纷纭皆未核其自序而误也其书宋艺文志作三卷世无本惟散见永乐大典者搜采尚得六十余条核其所记皆唐末朝野之故事与晁氏所云録唐大中后事者相合其中于将相之贤否藩镇之强弱以及文章吟咏神竒怪之事靡所不载多足与正史相防证观资治通鉴所载宣宗引对令狐绹李景让禀母训王师范拜县令王式驭乱卒诸事皆本是书则司马光亦极取之惟其纪刘鄩袭兖州一条以兖帅

为张姓而考之五代欧薛二史则当时兖帅实葛从周不免闻异词然要其大致可信者多与大唐载诸书摭拾委巷之谈者相去固悬絶矣胡应麟九流绪论乃以鄙浅讥之考应麟仍以崇逺为唐人不纠晁氏之误知未见其自序又取与刘基郁离子苏伯衡空同子相较是并不知为记事之书误侪诸立言之列明人诡薄好为大言以售欺不足信也谨裒缀编次分为二卷而以崇逺原序冠之简端以存其畧焉乾隆四十六年九月恭校上 总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅 总 校 官 【臣】 陆 费 墀 钦定四库全书 中朝故事卷上 南唐尉迟偓撰 大中皇帝多微行坊曲间跨驴重戴纵目四顾往往及暮方归大内近臣多谏陛下不合频出上曰吾要采访民间风俗事只如明皇帝未平内难已前在藩邸间出游城南韦杜之曲间行村落之舎遇王琚闲话果賛成大事吾是以要访人物焉一日到天街中道旁见一人状若军将坐槐下石上见上来遽起鞠躬而立上诘之云姓

赵淮南人也问之云闻杜琮相公出镇淮南欲往谒耳上曰旧识耶对云非旧识始往投诚上曰公闻杜公何如人也对曰杜是累朝元老圣上英明复委用之非偶然也上恱之诘曰懐中何有乃一牍述行止也上留之戒曰但留邸中伺候杜公必来奉召翌日上以状授邠公乃批云授淮南别勅押衙终身获厚禄焉其人感遇人皆称之 宣宗即宪皇少子也皇昆即穆宗也穆宗敬宗之后文宗武宗相次即位宣皇皆叔父也武宗初登极深忌焉一日防鞠于禁苑间武宗召上遥覩瞬目于中官仇士良跃马向前曰适有防王可下马士良命中官舆出军中奏云落马已不救矣寻请爲僧游行江表间防昌末中人请还京遂即位僖宗皇帝以咸通三年降诞十四年七月十九日即位年十二左军防军田令孜辅翊于朝僖宗呼爲阿父朝纲由已人无敢言每入对敭皆自备两牙盘果食便对御前从容良久而退以爲常式数年后扈从幸蜀转恣眦睚杀害孔多及翠华还京不敢侍从时令孜见陈敬瑄爲西川节度乃求爲监军而殂

昭宗皇帝即僖皇弟也咸通八年丁亥嵗降生文德元年三月即位春秋二十二体貌端明人望伟如也虽运钟艰险智量过人每与侍臣言论商较时政曾无厌倦干宁三年凤翔李茂贞与朝臣有隙将欲搆难犯干神京上乃顺动欲幸太原行止渭北华州韩建迎归郡中上郁郁不乐时登城西齐云眺望眀年秋制菩萨蛮词二首曰登楼遥望秦宫殿茫茫只见双飞燕渭水一条流千山与万丘逺烟笼碧陌上行人去何处是英雄迎孥归故宫又一曰飘飖且在三峯下秋风往往堪沾洒肠断忆仙宫朦胧烟雾中思

梦时时睡不语常如醉早晩是归期穹苍知不知上戊午年还京庚申岁以中官多凶恶欲去其用事者十一月五日爲左军军容刘季述右军王仲仙枢宻使王彦范薛齐偓拥禁兵唤诸道进奏官僞上表请上頥养逊位扶上出东内册德皇监国上明年正月一日反正诛四辈改天复元年十一月朱全忠领兵入河中四月冬节上又爲凤翔兵士拥幸政城朱全忠将兵迎驾围逼旹涉三载癸亥岁正月二十二日驾出朱全忠寨中乃还辇毂甲子岁全忠迎上幸洛四月改天祐元年八月十一日乃行簒逆寰海莫不寃痛也 京兆尹有生杀之柄然而清要之官多轻薄之目爲所由之司京国士子进士成名后便列清途屈指以期大用故事若登廊庙须曽扬歴于字人遂假途于长安万年之邑或驾在东洛亦爲河南洛阳之宰数月之后必迁居閤下京尹不可侔也两县令初欲莅事须谒谢京尹皆异常待之庭前铺置茵褥府史引一人投刺于尹前云某邑令某姓名讃两拜而已大尹降西廊迎之从容便就饭防府中遂爲体例

咸通中中书侍郎平章事刘瞻以清俭自守忠正佐时懿皇以同昌公主薨谢怒其医官韩宗绍等絷于霜台并亲属二三百人散系大理内外忧惧瞻上防切谏时路岩韦保衡恃宠忌之出瞻爲荆南节度使中外咸不平之翰林承防郑畋爲制词略曰早以文学叠中殊科风棱甚高恭愼无玷而又僻于亷洁不尚浮华安数畆之居仍非己有却四方之贿唯畏人知云云韦路大怒贬畋爲梧州刺史取十道图检见驩州去京万里乃谪瞻爲驩州司戸叅军 舎人李庾行诰词駮责深焉将欲加害时遇懿皇厌代僖皇初立用元臣萧仿佐佑大政仿举瞻自代又幽州节度使张公素上防理之韦路意乃止焉俄而路岩出爲益帅保衡又离相位召瞻爲康州刺史再授虢州瞻旋至湘江韦保衡南窜相遇于江中瞻家人齐登舟外诟骂之保衡约束家人无辞以对至贺州驿内伏法乃是数年前杀杨收閤子中榻上也瞻至湖南李庾方典是郡出迎于江次竹牌亭置酒瞻唱竹枝词送李庾蹑履过沟竹枝恨渠深女儿

庾慑怒乃上酒于瞻瞻命庾酬唱庾云不晓词间音律瞻投杯曰君应只解爲制词也是夕庾饮鸩而卒瞻至京俄入中书时宰相刘邺先与韦路相熟深有忧色方判盐铁乃于院中置防召瞻饮中寘毒而薨邺寻授淮南节度使僖皇于麟德殿置宴伶人有词曰刘公出典扬州庶事必应大治民瘼康防矣诸伶人皆倡和曰此真最药王菩萨也人皆哂之路岩即贬儋州百姓至江陵籍没家产不知纪极有蚊防一领轻宻如碧烟人疑其鲛鮹也及新州伏法 咸通中辅相崔彦昭兵部侍郎王凝乃外表兄弟也凝大中元年进士及第来年彦昭犹下第因访凝凝衣见之崔甚恚凝又戏之曰君却好应明经科举也彦昭忿怒而出三年乃登第懿皇朝多自夏官侍郎判盐铁即秉钧轴一旦凝拜是官决意入相彦昭陷之后数月之间盐鐡中有隳坏凝罢职朝廷以彦昭爲之半载而入相彦昭母乃命多制鞋履谓侍婢曰王氏妹必与王侍郎同窜逐吾要伴小妹同行也彦昭闻之泣拜其母谢曰必无此事王凝竟免其责也

古者五行官守皆不失其职声色香味俱能别之賛皇公李德裕博达之士也居庙廊日有亲知奉使于京口李曰还日金山下扬子江中泠水与取一壶来其人举棹日醉而忘之泛舟上石城下方忆及汲一瓶于江中归京献之李公饮后惊讶非常曰江表水味有异于顷岁矣此水颇似建业石城下水其人谢过不敢隐也有亲知授舒州牧李谓之曰到彼郡日天柱峯茶可惠三数角其人献之数十斤李不受退还明年罢郡用意精求获数角投之賛皇阅之而受曰此茶可消酒肉毒乃命烹一瓯沃于肉食以银合闭之诘旦同开视其肉已化爲水矣众伏其广识也 旧说海中有水贯于新罗国色清而甘或彼国怠于进奉中华则彼水浊而无味又岭南茘枝明皇幸蜀后江南之人使罕及此果亦彼中不稔干符中僖皇在蜀洞庭柑橘东都嘉庆李睦仁柿亦味醋而澁北省官往日遗补每上防諌诤多谢罪立誓词右补阙与左拾遗结状故中朝士人重右补阙左拾遗也

前朝宰相罕有不左降者唯徐商持政公数十年不曽有累其子齐国公彦若亦以忠于上和于众竟无贬谪之祸 卢耽自进士登科后出将入相四十九年不曽称前衘皆从此任受于彼宰相堂饭常人多不敢食郑延昌在相位一日本欲食次其弟延济来遂与之同食延济手擎馎饦及数口椀自手中坠地遂中风痹一夕而卒太常卿初上寺内以雅乐全作而呈之少卿初上以半呈之 搢绅子弟皆怯于尚公主葢以帝戚强盛公主自置羣僚以至庄宅库轝尽多主吏宅中各有院落聚防不同公主多亲戚聚宴或出盘游驸马不得与之相见凡出入间婢仆不敢顾盼公主则恣行所爲往往数朝不一相见唯于琮相国所尚广德公主则贤和不同乃懿皇亲妹于琮遭韦路所逐同到昭州于公累起被中官赐药酒公主诟骂夺而掷之常侍于公手执公腰带而坐凢所经歴州郡官吏不敢叅迎道途肩舁门相对而行果寻被诏却还辇毂授太子少傅次除右仆射所谓公主之力也
宫苑之间八节游从固多名目每岁樱桃熟时两军各择日排宴祗行幸谓之行从盛陈歌乐以至尽日倡优百戏水陆无不具陈在处堆积樱桃以充看翫也同州有长春宫其间园林繁茂花木无所不有芳菲长如三春节矣中书政事堂后有五房堂候官共十五人每嵗都酿醵钱十五万贯秋间于坊曲税四区大宅鳞次相列取便脩装徧栽花药至牡丹开日请四相到其中并家人亲戚日迎达官至暮娱乐教坊声妓无不来者恩赐酒食亦无虚日中官驱髙车大马而至以取金帛优赏花落而罢

京辇自黄巢退后脩葺残毁之处鎭州王家有一儿俗号王酒胡居于上都巨有钱物纳钱三十万贯助修朱雀门上又诏重脩安国寺毕亲降车辇以设大斋乃十二撞新钟舍钱一万贯令诸大臣各取意击之上曰有人能舍钱一千贯文者却打一槌斋罢王酒胡半醉入来径上钟楼连打一百下便于西市运钱十万贯入寺韩建防母寻访松楸之地有术士云只有一穴可置大叚钱物亦乃不乆而散若华州境内即莫加于此也建乃于兹葬母明年大驾来幸三峯四海之人罔不辐凑建乃广收商税二载之后有见钱九百万贯后三年尽爲朱全忠所有 两军所置街巡禁止军中凶暴若百姓爲盗鬬即属京兆府并两县捕贼司军人百姓不相叅杂天下亦如此天街两畔槐树俗号爲槐衙曲江池畔多柳亦号爲柳衙意谓其成行列如排衙也 每岁上巳日许宫女于兴庆宫内大同殿前与骨肉相见纵其问讯家眷更相赠遗一日之内人有千万有初到亲戚便相见者有及暮而呼唤姓第不至者涕泣而去岁岁如此

华清宫汤泉内天宝中刻石爲座及芙蓉闻说到今犹在屋木亦有全者骊山多飞禽名阿滥堆明皇帝御玉笛采其声翻爲曲子名焉左右皆传唱之播于逺近人竞以笛效吹故词人张祐诗曰红树萧萧閤半开上皇曽幸此宫来至今风俗骊山下村笛犹吹阿滥堆 中朝故事卷上 钦定四库全书 中朝故事卷下 南唐尉迟偓撰 古有豢龙氏长安有豢龙戸观水即知龙色目有无悉知之懿皇朝龙戸上言龙池中走失两条往关东寻访数十日东都魏王池中见之取而归阙经华州时李讷爲华州刺史讷父名建杓向与白居易相善讷爲人正直闻得龙来大以爲虚妄命就公府视之则于一小瓶子中倒于盆内乃二细鳅鱼也讷怒目曰何以爲验其人对曰验非难也请于地中凿一穴阔一尺已而注水其间鳅投水内鱼到水中相趂旋转尾触穴四隅随触而陷水亦暴涨逡廵穴已阔数尺其人谘讷云恐穴更广即难制也遂搦入瓶中讷方竒之厚赠钱帛携归辇下

徐彦若弟彦枢大中末遇京国中元夜观灯于坊曲间夜深有一人前揖徐公因同行谓徐公曰君贵人也他年贤兄必爲辅弼之官若近十年即须请退去京五千里外方免难也不尔当有祸患行及一小巷口其人曰某在此巷内居别日请相访遂分路而去经旬日彦枢行及其巷乃访之并无人居行十步余有一小神祠外路已穷尽于是谒其神见土偶宛是夜中所覩者含笑相视彦枢记之光化末彦枢官至左諌议大夫兄方居宰辅遂话于兄时四方皆爲豪杰所据唯有广南是嗣薛王知柔爲节度使彦若遂请出广州昭皇授以节钺而去果免患难

宣皇朝有术士董元素自江南来人言能役使神上闻之召见状貌甚异帝谓左右曰斯人不可测也留于翰林中宿洎夜召与语曰闻公颇有神术今南中柑橘正熟卿能致之否元素对曰此小事请安一合于御榻前数刻间有微风入幕元素乃啓其合柑子满其中奏曰此江陵枝江县柑子也逺处取恐迟上尝之甚惊叹谓之曰卿要物应不难也元素曰若非奉天命臣何敢自取自取必有阴谴明日上命一内家小儿以银笛吹之夜上高树宣元素从容闻其声上曰近有此怪卿爲朕逐之可否元素笑曰此小儿耳乃书一符飞之顷刻乃不闻其声明日唤小儿询之云方吹次似有人于口中拨去黒

中无计求也上又以十余片令怀上树踵前吹之宣元素听元素吐气少许其声遂絶上再三怪之明日唤问乃是被风吹落宫墙外无由到上又令人于后苑作一地穴如屋防烛于其间使数人鸣鼓白元素曰又有此妖卿可逐之复飞符良乆乃不闻其声翌日上责穴中人曰方击鼔次奉中使宣防不用击也其夕又穴鸣之戒曰任闻宣传不可止也复不闻声上明日问其由奏曰昨夜陛下亲到穴止约臣遂不敢违命上曰今夕更爲纵是我来亦不可止也复不闻声隔日奏曰有一赤龙入穴人皆惊走所以然也宣皇骇之异常敬重前后异术不可尽记赐赉孔多半年后坚辞归江南乃放去不知其终
李思齐者常着绿戴席帽于京辇状貌若三十许人每阅市场登酒肆逢人即与相善令狐楚闻之召至宅语言非常人楚子绹侍立覩之亦觉其异云在昊天观安下明日楚令人覔之无踪咸通中绹爲淮南节度使已逾三十年矣门吏于市肆见思齐貌若当时惊而白绹绹亦惊使邀之拜爲丈人谓绹曰何衰老如是绹复再拜留宿府中不住云在紫极宫安下去而不复来有人复一见在酒楼上绹又令访之竟不来莫知所去叚文昌贞元中在西川爲南康王韦臯宾从臯薨后遭刘辟遂爲外邑佐官
高崇文复劒南召居旧职文昌再三谢之崇文曰君非乆在卑位也指已座下椅子谓之曰此椅子犹不足与君坐遽请归阙行至兴元一山寺中有老僧指庭前梅树曰君去日既逢梅脸绽来时应见杏花开及抵京华屡迁爵秩数年后拜益州节度使经兴元至往日僧院覩庭中杏花方盛访其僧已卒文昌追思之感怆爲之设斋而去文昌孙安节爲人厚重言未尝虚发每云天复中避乱出京至商山中逆旅见一老妇人无一半头坐床心缉麻运手甚熟其儿妇在侧言广明庚子岁巢宼入京爲贼所伤
自鼻一半已上并随刅去有人以药封裹之时不死两日亦如往者后微动手足眷属以米饮灌口中乆而无恙今已二十余年矣人间有此异事安节又云长安多凶宅无人敢居街东有宅堂中有一青面如靛色双目若火其面满五间堂屋中人呼爲大青面街西有宅头防中亦有青面可以一间屋中人呼爲小青面安节少年因冷节与侪数人筑气毬落于此宅中斟酌不逺于壁隙见在细草内安节与众穿壁入去取毬数步间试窥防中果见其面满屋下泛眼视诸人乃一时奔出莫敢取其毬也

咸通中有幻术者不知其姓名于坊曲爲戏挈一小儿年十岁已来有刀截下头卧于地上以头安置之遂乞钱云活此儿子众竞与之乃叱一声其儿便走起明日又如此聚人千万钱多后叱儿不起其人乃谢诸看人云某乍到京国未获拜所有高手在此致此小术不行且望纵之某当拜爲师父言讫叱其小儿不起俄有廵吏执之言汝杀人须赴公府其人曰千万人中某固难逃窜然某更有异术请且观之 就法亦不晩乃于一函内取一子以刀划开臂上搯子于其中又设法起其儿子无效斯须露其臂已生一小甜子在臂上乃曰某不欲杀人愿高手放斯小儿起实爲幸矣复叱之不兴其人嗟叹曰不免杀人也以刀削其甜落喝一声小儿乃起如故众中有一僧头歘然堕地乃收拾戏具并小儿入布囊中结于背上仰面吐气一道如疋练上冲空中忽引手攀缘而上丈余而没遂失所在其僧竟身首异处焉

西明寺中有僧名德真过海欲往新罗舟至海中山岛畔避风与同舟一道流行其岛屿间见泉水一中有赤鲤一头道士取之不得乃念呪禹步获之僧云海中异物不可拘也道士曰海神吾无惧僧苦求免之投于波内乃往海东明年僧还京复寓西明寺乃能卜射言事无不中者由是谒请如市一二年间获缗不知其数一旦有客诣之见小栢木神堂内幡花填其中客以手扪其中得一小儿长数寸朱衣朱冠眉目如画状似欲语忽脱手飞去空中而不见其僧叹惋久之乃诟骂逐其客客惧走避之经月闻其僧言其事皆无凭也王鲔者凝之兄也多异术有相知多智爲使往宣州推事谓鲔曰有何饯行相赠鲔出一小囊

其间如弹丸不知何物也谓之曰可长结在身边无忘也既到宣州推事月余日昼寝于驿防内睡中转身爲弹子所隐脇下痛极因跃下牀就外观之屋梁忽折落于榻上枕席有声震骇驿内使人免兹难也康軿着剧谈录亦载鲔有异术中书令韦昭度方秉机衡中外趋附者千万忽有老僧来谒昭度方在道院独坐覩其僧颇异之僧曰令公祸将及矣能随贫道去否特来相迎耳昭度恍然失色亦甚惧焉白僧曰某当权已久深虑祸生甚欲逺行然畧须辞别家眷其僧不许昭度须请入焉及至堂中长幼聚哭云无信妄说拽其衣裾移时昭度脱身趋道院已失其僧矣询诸阍吏无有见者两月间遂遇难与表弟李磎同破家也

代说郑畋是胎其母卒后与其父亚再合而生畋初亚未达旅游诸处留其妻并一婢在山观中女冠院侧及归妻已卒询其婢婢曰娘子将欲产卧之夕闻空中有语曰汝须出观外无触汚吾清境不然吾当杀汝妻祝曰某妇人也出无所归愿圣者悯念及五更分娠后乃殒絶观内道众爲殡于墙外野田中矣亚以钱酒往酹之是夜梦妻曰某命未尽合与君生贵子无何以触汚道院爲神灵所杀从此向南十里有一僧院其间秪有一僧年可五十来此奇士也君可往求之僧必拒讳但再三哀鸣祈之当得再奉

箕箒也及寤不以爲信次夕又再梦之语如初亚于是趋其院果见彼桑门初谒之亦喜亚遂告之殊不管顾曰我即凡人也偶出家耳岂能主幽防之间事乎亚复恳祈之僧怒以拄杖驱击亚甘其辱连日不去夕亦不寝僧乃许之曰汝既心坚若此俟吾寻访之乃坐入定半夜后起谓亚曰事谐矣天曙但先归吾当送来亚其夕归观三更中闻外戸人语即引妻来言本身已坏此即魂也善相保守嘱之而去其事宛如平生但恶明处三二年间乃生畋又数岁妻乃辞去言年数已尽合当决去涕泗而别俄不知所之 咸通初有布衣爨忘记其名到京辇云黔巫间来王公之第以羊挺炭三十斤自出小锯并小刀斧剪截其炭叠成二楼数刻乃成散药末于上下用火烧之药引火势斯须即通彻二楼光明赫然望其檐宇窓戸雕楹刻桷并阑槛罔不周俻又有飞桥连接二楼有人物男女若来往其上移时后炭渐飞成灰方无所覩懿皇闻之召入宫禁乆而不知所之

李琮爲湖南观察使渔者献鲤鱼一头长数尺琮命家人烹之鱼腹得印一面文曰衡山县印琮令防吏索衡山县近文书看其印分明乃遣召衡山令使擕印来及到阅之果然新铸也琮屏人诘之宰邑者伏罪首曰旧印爲恶人窃去某与主吏并忧刑戮所以潜命工匠爲之今则唯俟死命也琮悯之爲秘其事碎新印令赍旧印归县罕有知之者 邠公杜琮人臣福夀少有其伦日常五飡以爲常式一日之费皆至万钱夜间亦是一食暮年有医工谘曰相公不宜夜食恐臓腑壅滞以致疾琮笑曰吾六十余年如此矣有何患哉京西有客见人牧羊徧满山垅不知几千万口客诘之自何而来荅曰来自鄜夏供相公食耳指顾之际转首恍然并无所覩乃知神灵所授也 中朝故事卷下
